title: The Hidden Cost of Cheap Fashion
summary: Argumentative B1 text — passive voice, conditional, AWL vocabulary.
text:
Every year, the average European buys about 26 kilograms of clothing. Most of it ends up in landfills within a year. The fashion industry has become one of the most polluting industries in the world, accounting for around 10% of global carbon emissions — more than international flights and shipping combined.

The reason behind this is what experts call "fast fashion": cheap, disposable clothes designed to be worn briefly and thrown away. Shops release new collections every few weeks instead of every season. To keep prices low, factories are often located in countries where labor regulations are weak and workers earn less than two euros a day.

If we want to mitigate the impact, we have several options. We can buy fewer but better-quality items. We can shop second-hand. We can repair instead of replacing. None of these solutions is glamorous, but eventually they would shift the market.

Of course, the responsibility cannot be placed only on the consumer. Governments must regulate the industry, and brands have to be held accountable. Notwithstanding these structural issues, individual choices still matter — they send a clear signal about what we value.

questions.q: What percentage of global carbon emissions does fashion produce?
What is 'fast fashion' according to the text?
What is NOT mentioned as a personal solution?
What does the author say about responsibility?
options: Both consumers and governments share responsibility

questions.q: What is the author's main argument?
options: Cheap fashion has serious costs we don't see
Everyone should make their own clothes
